ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general tempura class (FOODON_03302050); no specific bell pepper tempura subclass exists. Closest_parent used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated bell pepper tempura entry. Code A00GJ for battered and fried vegetables is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for bell pepper tempura or vegetable tempura as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of bell pepper tempura consists of approximately 5–6 pieces, weighing around 100 g, often served as a side dish or appetizer in a Japanese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Bell Pepper Tempura — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for Bell Pepper Tempura sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080003). All seven core and secondary nutrient fields are taken from this source at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for vegetable tempura (NDB No. 35188) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bell pepper tempura. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the GI of wheat-flour-based tempura batter analogous to other battered and deep-fried vegetable products (GI range 60–70) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Bell pepper itself has negligible GI impact; the dominant GI driver is the refined wheat flour and cornstarch batter. Glycemic load per 100 g serving = (65 × 16.93) / 100 = 11.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 72 mg/100 g — well within low-sodium threshold
hypertension.guidance: Bell pepper tempura has a low sodium content of 72 mg/100 g, making the dish itself acceptable for hypertension management; however, patients must strictly avoid or minimise tempura dipping sauce (tentsuyu), which contains soy sauce and mirin and can contribute 400–600 mg sodium per 30 mL serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined batter carbohydrate 16.93 g/100 g with low fibre 1.52 g/100 g and estimated GI 65
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined wheat flour and cornstarch batter raises the glycaemic index to approximately 65 and delivers a glycaemic load of ~11 per 100 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 3–4 pieces, pair with high-fibre vegetables and lean protein to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and avoid the sugar-containing dipping sauce.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 9.55 g/100 g including estimated saturated fat ~1.4 g and trace trans fat ~0.2 g from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying in vegetable oil contributes 9.55 g total fat per 100 g, with an estimated 1.4 g saturated fat and approximately 0.2 g trans fat from oil degradation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 4–5 pieces and prefer raw or grilled bell pepper preparations to reduce atherogenic fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nightshade vegetable, deep-frying AGEs, and trace trans fat — all pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bell pepper is a nightshade vegetable that may exacerbate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; combined with advanced glycation end products (AGEs) generated during deep-frying and an estimated 0.2 g/100 g trans fat, this dish is not recommended for individuals with active rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bell Pepper Tempura (ピーマンの天ぷら, Pīman no tempura) is a Japanese dish consisting of fresh bell pepper slices coated in a light, airy tempura batter made from wheat flour, cornstarch, egg, and cold water, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crisp. The dish originates from the Japanese tempura tradition, which was introduced to Japan by Portuguese missionaries in the 16th century and has since become a cornerstone of Japanese cuisine. Bell pepper tempura is commonly served as a side dish (okazu) or as part of a mixed tempura platter (moriawase) alongside other vegetables and seafood, accompanied by tentsuyu dipping sauce and grated daikon radish. The texture is characteristically light and crispy on the outside with a tender, slightly sweet bell pepper interior. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 2.61 g protein, 9.55 g fat, and 16.93 g carbohydrate, with the carbohydrate load driven primarily by the refined batter rather than the bell pepper itself. Dietary fibre is modest at 1.52 g/100 g and sodium is low at 72 mg/100 g. The estimated glycaemic index is 65 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 11 per 100 g serving. Clinically, the dish is low in sodium and suitable for hypertension when consumed without dipping sauce, but the refined batter raises glycaemic concern for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The deep-frying process introduces saturated fat (~1.4 g/100 g) and trace trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), which are relevant for cardiovascular disease management. Bell pepper is a nightshade vegetable and may be pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis patients. The dish is vegetarian and halal but contains gluten and egg allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in bell pepper tempura?
Can diabetics eat bell pepper tempura?
Is tempura bell pepper high in carbs?
Is bell pepper tempura suitable for hypertension?
Does bell pepper tempura contain gluten?
What is the GI of tempura?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080003) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central vegetable tempura analogue. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous battered vegetable products. Japanese local name verified against standard Japanese culinary terminology.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for battered and fried vegetables (closest approved-source analogue) as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat breakdown for this item. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is an estimate based on typical deep-frying oil degradation values; no direct measurement is available from HPB or USDA for this specific preparation. 3. Potassium (150 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for raw bell pepper adjusted for cooking losses; HPB does not report potassium for this item. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid profile for bell pepper tempura as a composite dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 5. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on wheat-flour-based battered and fried vegetable products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for bell pepper tempura exists in the approved GI literature. 6. FoodOn code FOODON_03302050 is used as a broader class anchor; no exact FoodOn subclass for bell pepper tempura exists. FoodEx2 code A00GJ is used as the closest defensible analogue for battered and fried vegetables.
